topic: A family member announces they're converting to a different religion
raw_output:
[story 1]

The words hung in the air, heavy and sharp, like shards of glass underfoot. "I'm leaving," Aunt Carol announced, her voice unnervingly calm, as if discussing the weather. I stood frozen by the kitchen counter, the ceramic mug trembling so violently I had to grip the edge to keep it from shattering. My breath hitched, a shallow, frantic thing in my chest. I couldn't process it. Leaving? Just like that? My mind spun, a dizzying carousel of impossible scenarios flashing before my eyes. I started pacing, a tight, jerky motion, my hands twisting the fabric of my shirt until the threads dug painfully into my skin. Every sound—the hum of the refrigerator, the distant traffic—seemed amplified, pressing in on me until I felt like I might actually combust. I just wanted the noise to stop, the sheer, overwhelming impossibility of the statement to dissolve.

[story 2]

"So, I've decided to follow the path of the Ascended now," my brother said, leaning back in his chair with an air of profound serenity. The shift in his tone, the absolute certainty in his gaze, hit me like a physical blow. My stomach lurched, a sickening, churning sensation that made me want to double over. I couldn't speak. My mouth felt dry, coated in a thick, tasteless residue. I began to rub my temples vigorously, pressing hard enough to leave throbbing indentations. My vision swam at the edges, colors blurring into streaks of frantic light. I took a stumbling step backward, bumping into the doorframe, and let out a sound that was more a sharp gasp than a word, a sound entirely devoid of my usual composure. The world seemed tilted, unstable, and every decision felt monumental and terrifyingly wrong.

[story 3]

When Dad finally uttered the news—a quiet declaration about changing his faith—the silence that followed was deafening. I felt a sudden, intense pressure building behind my eyes, a tightening sensation that made focusing on anything impossible. My hands started shaking uncontrollably, a fine, rapid tremor that made holding my phone a monumental effort. I stared at the floor, tracing the pattern of the wood grain with my eyes, trying to anchor myself to something solid. But everything felt fluid, wrong. A hot flush crept up my neck, and I could feel my pulse hammering against my ribs with such force it was almost painful. I started to breathe too fast, shallow gasps that didn't seem to reach my lungs properly. I needed air, needed distance, needed anything to break this suffocating closeness of the moment.
